local_names.romanized_local: Shui Niu Cheng Ji Chi (Mandarin romanization)

region_of_origin: United States (Buffalo, New York); served in Singapore via Western-casual restaurants
food_subgroup: Poultry — deep-fried and sauced chicken wings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002703 covers chicken wing food product as a closest parent; no specific buffalo-style sauced wing term exists in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chicken wings as a category; no buffalo-sauce preparation sub-code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for buffalo-style chicken wings exists; snomed_ct_code set to null.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of buffalo wings is approximately 6 pieces, weighing around 240 g, typically served with celery sticks and blue cheese or ranch dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken wing, frozen, glazed, with sauce, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked chicken wings with hot sauce (NDB No. 05062 and related entries for sauced/fried chicken wings). Buffalo wings are deep-fried chicken wings tossed in cayenne-butter hot sauce; the USDA SR Legacy entry for fried chicken wing with skin provides the closest approved nutritional anchor. Sauce contribution (hot sauce, butter) is incorporated into the per-100g estimate. Values represent a composite of USDA SR Legacy fried chicken wing with skin (NDB 05062) and hot sauce contribution, consistent with published literature on buffalo wing composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Buffalo wings are a high-protein, high-fat, very low-carbohydrate food (approximately 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). No direct GI measurement exists for buffalo wings in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 5 (effectively negligible) by analogy with other high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked chicken preparations, consistent with the principle that foods with negligible available carbohydrate have a GI near zero. Atkinson et al. (2021) and the University of Sydney GI Database confirm that protein- and fat-dominant foods with minimal carbohydrate content have GI values in the range 0–10. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (5 × 8.4) / 100 ≈ 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~680 mg per 100 g; ~1632 mg per standard 240 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit buffalo wing consumption due to the very high sodium load from hot sauce and seasoning, which can significantly elevate blood pressure; a single restaurant serving may provide over 65% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high saturated fat and caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Buffalo wings have a negligible glycemic impact due to very low carbohydrate content, making them unlikely to cause acute postprandial glucose spikes; however, the high saturated fat content (~5.8 g per 100 g) and caloric density (~290 kcal per 100 g) may contribute to insulin resistance and weight gain with regular consumption, which are key risk factors for Type 2 Diabetes management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Buffalo wings present multiple cardiovascular risk factors including elevated saturated fat (~5.8 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g from frying), cholesterol (~95 mg/100 g), and very high sodium (~680 mg/100 g); individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should consume this food only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Buffalo wings are deep-fried chicken wing sections — typically the drumette and flat — that are tossed in a tangy, spicy sauce made from cayenne-based hot sauce (such as Frank's RedHot) and melted butter. The dish originated at the Anchor Bar in Buffalo, New York, USA, in 1964 and has since become a globally popular bar snack and appetizer. In Singapore, buffalo wings are served at Western-casual restaurants, sports bars, and international chain outlets, and appear on field survey under various restaurant menus. The wings are characterised by a crispy, golden-brown exterior from deep-frying, a juicy and tender interior, and a bold, tangy-spicy flavour profile from the hot sauce coating. They are commonly served with celery sticks and a cooling blue cheese or ranch dipping sauce to balance the heat. Nutritionally, buffalo wings are high in protein (~22.5 g/100 g), high in fat (~20.5 g/100 g including saturated fat), and very low in carbohydrates (~3.5 g/100 g), resulting in a low glycemic index. The sodium content is notably high (~680 mg/100 g) due to the hot sauce and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Despite the low GI, the caloric density and saturated fat content warrant moderation for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. The cuisine tag in the source data listed Malaysian, but buffalo wings are an American dish served in a Singaporean context; the cuisine classification has been corrected to American.
common_user_queries: How many calories are in buffalo wings?
Are buffalo wings high in sodium?
What is the GI of buffalo wings?
How much protein is in buffalo wings?
What sauce is used in buffalo wings?
Buffalo wings vs fried chicken nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry exists for buffalo wings as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried chicken wings with skin, with hot sauce and butter contribution incorporated. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein profile of the dish, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database principles. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved primary source provides a direct buffalo wings entry.
notes: vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source for this fried chicken wing preparation; vitamin D data for this specific entry is absent from the source database and cannot be reliably estimated without an approved direct measurement. 1. The input record listed cuisine as 'Malaysian' with tags including 'Curry Rice', 'Nasi Lemak', 'Indian', and 'Butter Chicken'. These tags appear to reflect the restaurant's broader menu rather than the dish itself. Buffalo wings are an American dish; cuisine has been corrected to 'American' and region_of_origin updated accordingly. 2. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided in accordance with the Singaporean dish multilingual rule, as this dish is served in Singapore. 3. trans_fat_g is estimated at 0.2 g/100 g from deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or standard frying oils; this is an approximation. 4. The is_halal flag is set to true conditionally — buffalo wings made with halal-certified chicken and without alcohol-based hot sauce are halal; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 5. GI is set to 5 (low) as an analogous estimate for this very low-carbohydrate, high-protein food; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.4 based on approximately 8.4 g carbohydrate in a 240 g serving.
